key_insight: Raising argon flow from 1600 to 1800 L/h during air plasma spraying produced thicker, denser NiCrFeBSiC coatings with better tribological performance.
engineering_relevance: Optimizing argon flow is a simple process lever for improving coating quality, wear resistance, and deposition efficiency in thermal spray manufacturing.
method: Air plasma sprayed self-fluxing NiCrFeBSiC metal-ceramic coatings
Compared argon flow rates of 1600 and 1800 L/h
Evaluated microstructure, microhardness, elastic modulus, ball-on-disk friction, and scratch adhesion
evidence_signal: At 1800 L/h, coating thickness increased by about 2×, porosity decreased by about 2×, and the friction coefficient dropped to approximately 0.107, with high adhesion confirmed by scratch testing.
watch_item: Results are based on only two argon-flow settings, so broader process optimization and long-term durability should be validated before scale-up.
paper_title: EFFECT OF ARGON FLOW RATE DURING AIR PLASMA SPRAYING ON THE STRUCTURE AND PROPERTIES OF NICRFEBSIC COATINGS
